iteration_notes: Replaced Mike Collins foreign-policy sourcing with a direct official House press release on Iran and raised confidence to high based on direct candidate statements.
Verified Jon Ossoff's Tech & AI press release exists on his official Senate website and retained the stance with confirmed sourcing.
Expanded Derek Dooley's summary to explicitly note that his campaign has offered limited issue-specific policy detail beyond his Georgia First Contract and outsider/accountability themes.

Searched for additional Derek Dooley issue material in campaign and news sources; where detailed policy positions remained unavailable, clarified those gaps in neutral language rather than overstating positions.
Added top donor entries for Mike Collins using OpenSecrets top contributors data for the 2023-2024 cycle, each with source objects.

Added a note in Derek Dooley's top_donors limitation by populating named donor entries from available FEC candidate receipts context was not directly retrievable here; because itemized donor names were not verifiable from fetched source text, the array remains empty and the summary/notes reflect that limitation.

Reviewed polling availability and added a note that, as of late March 2026, reviewed public polling located for this race was sparse and the profile includes the verifiable Emerson poll while noting the field may evolve.
Improved the race description to note the Republican field could still change as the election approaches.
Enhanced Jon Ossoff's H.R. 6938 voting-record description with more specific context from Congress.gov/GovTrack bill summaries.

Reframed Mike Collins' Tech & AI issue to clarify that available sourcing reflects campaign behavior and blockchain/technology-related legislative activity more than a defined AI-regulation platform, and lowered confidence to low.
Added an additional Buddy Carter reproductive-rights source from his official House materials to give broader context to his anti-abortion position.
Verified all candidates still cover the 12 canonical issues and preserved required voting_record field names and vote values.
title: 2026 U.S. Senate election in Georgia
description: Georgia’s 2026 U.S. Senate race is centered on incumbent Democrat Jon Ossoff’s bid for a second term in a state that has become highly competitive in federal elections. The Republican field includes Rep. Buddy Carter, Rep. Mike Collins, and former football coach Derek Dooley, with early polling showing Ossoff ahead of each potential GOP opponent while Collins leads the Republican primary field. The race is likely to hinge on turnout, candidate ideology, and whether Republicans unify behind one nominee in a state that President Trump carried in 2024.

candidates.name: Jon Ossoff
candidates.summary: Jon Ossoff is the incumbent Democratic U.S. senator from Georgia and filed his 2026 re-election paperwork on March 27, 2026. His campaign reported $25.6 million cash on hand at the end of 2025, and his office has continued issuing Senate press releases on issues including TSA funding, Georgia ports, veterans, and national security.

Healthcare.stance: Ossoff has recently emphasized lowering prescription drug prices, expanding access to care, and limiting insurer practices that delay treatment. In January 2026, he said a law he backed lowered prices for 10 Medicare drugs, and in February 2026 he backed the bipartisan Safe Step Act to curb insurer step-therapy delays and make access to medically necessary treatment more transparent.

sources.title: NEWS: Sen. Ossoff Helps Lower Prices of 10 Prescription Drugs in 2026
Sen. Ossoff Cosponsors Bipartisan Bill to Stop Insurance Companies from Delaying Needed Medical Care
Bipartisan Bill Championed by Sen. Ossoff to Help Georgia Children Get Faster Medical Care Becomes Law

Economy.stance: Ossoff continues to emphasize housing affordability, infrastructure investment, and workforce development as core economic priorities. In his 2025 five-year report, he said he launched an investigation into large out-of-state corporations buying single-family homes in Georgia and won bipartisan support for a federal housing affordability investigation. He also said he has championed Georgia’s economic development, protected infrastructure investments, and opposed trade wars that raise prices for small businesses and consumers.

sources.title: Five-Year Report – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

Climate/Energy.stance: Ossoff has continued to emphasize clean-energy investment and domestic manufacturing, including support for solar and other energy projects tied to jobs and supply chains.

sources.title: U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

Reproductive Rights.stance: Ossoff has publicly supported abortion access, contraception access, and IVF. In March 2026, supporters and campaign-aligned messaging highlighted his work to safeguard reproductive health care, protect access to contraception, and support families seeking IVF treatment.

sources.title: Reproductive Freedom for All Endorses Georgia Senator Jon Ossoff for Reelection
Sen. Ossoff Pushing to Protect Women's Access to Contraception in Georgia

Immigration.stance: Continues to focus on oversight of immigration detention conditions and abuse allegations; on March 26, 2026, he launched an investigation into alleged abuse at the "Alligator Alcatraz" detention site, saying it may rise to the level of torture.

sources.title: Press Releases – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff
Press Releases – Page 2 – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

Guns & Safety.stance: Ossoff continues to emphasize gun safety and public safety, including support for stronger gun-safety measures and criticism of federal actions he says undermine public safety and civil liberties.

sources.title: GIFFORDS PAC endorses Sen. Jon Ossoff for reelection in Georgia
Ossoff: Republican Senate Candidates Must Call for Independent Investigation, Fight for Civil Liberties

Foreign Policy.stance: On March 18, 2026, Ossoff pressed the Director of National Intelligence on the imminent threat posed by Iran; on February 28, 2026, he criticized Donald Trump over U.S. air action involving Iran and emphasized congressional oversight of military action.

sources.title: Press Releases – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff
Press Releases – Page 2 – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

Social Justice.stance: Ossoff continues to frame his agenda around civil liberties and human rights, including oversight and criticism of federal enforcement actions he says violate Americans’ rights.

sources.title: Ossoff: Republican Senate Candidates Must Call for Independent Investigation, Fight for Civil Liberties
Ossoff: Republican Senate Candidates Must Fight for Civil Liberties, Stand With American People

Education.stance: Ossoff’s education message remains focused on workforce-oriented education and job training. In August 2025, he introduced the Skilled Workforce Act to encourage private investment in high schools, career academies, and technical colleges, saying technical colleges, community colleges, and skills training are the foundation for Georgia’s middle class. He also said the bill is meant to help Georgians get job training and hire Georgians.

sources.title: Sen. Ossoff Introduces Bill to Help Georgians Unlock Job Training Opportunities Across Georgia

Tech & AI.stance: Ossoff has recently emphasized oversight and guardrails for government use of AI. In February 2026, he joined an inquiry into the Defense Department's planned use of xAI's Grok, citing concerns about antisemitic content, Holocaust denial, racist ideologies, and deepfake child pornography.

sources.title: Sen. Ossoff, Colleagues Launch Inquiry into Department of Defense’s Use of AI Software Known for Antisemitic Content

Election Reform.stance: Ossoff is emphasizing voting rights and election administration rather than stricter voting restrictions; in March 2026 he publicly framed Republican election-funding fights around protecting federal workers and democratic institutions, and his campaign-facing messaging continues to center Georgia voters and democratic participation.

sources.title: Georgia – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff
Ahead of 2026 campaign, Ossoff says 'Georgia will bow to no king'

Local Issues.stance: Ossoff’s latest Georgia-focused agenda emphasizes local infrastructure, public safety, housing affordability, water systems, and constituent services; in March 2026 he highlighted funding for drinking water, stormwater, ports, housing, public safety, and community projects across Georgia.

sources.title: Georgia – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff
Five-Year Report – U.S. Senator for Georgia Jon Ossoff

candidates.name: Buddy Carter
candidates.summary: Buddy Carter is a Republican U.S. representative from Georgia’s 1st District and a candidate for the 2026 U.S. Senate race. He entered the contest in May 2025 and has continued campaigning while also using his House platform to criticize Democrats on federal funding and other issues.

Healthcare.stance: Carter has highlighted maternal health legislation, saying his Preventing Maternal Deaths Reauthorization Act would improve health outcomes for mothers and expand access to maternal health resources. Reporting on the 2026 Senate race also says he opposed renewing Affordable Care Act subsidies, with Republicans arguing those subsidies are financially unsustainable.

sources.title: Carter bill preventing maternal deaths and improving health outcomes for mothers heads to Senate
Democrats criticize Georgia US Senate challengers for end of health insurance subsidies

Economy.stance: Carter’s economic message continues to center on tax cuts, domestic production, reshoring, and protectionist trade policy. In March 2026, his campaign and congressional materials continued to frame him as supporting Trump’s economic agenda, including tariffs and policies aimed at boosting American workers and manufacturing. He also publicly defended Trump’s tariff authority after a Supreme Court ruling, saying the decision undercut the president’s ability to defend American workers.

sources.title: Trump's Home-Run Economic Policies Delivered for Georgians in ...
Carter statement on SCOTUS tariff ruling

Climate/Energy.stance: Carter backs an all-of-the-above energy strategy centered on increasing domestic production, promoting nuclear and other reliable energy sources, and cutting permitting and regulatory barriers. His House energy page says this approach is aimed at energy independence, lower prices, and job creation.

sources.title: Energy : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter

Reproductive Rights.stance: Carter's public materials reflect an anti-abortion position. His office's issue page is titled Protecting Life, and House materials also say he supported the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act and other anti-abortion measures.

sources.title: Protecting Life : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter
Representative Buddy Carter - Defending the right to life with the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act

Immigration.stance: Remains strongly enforcement-focused; on February 9, 2026, he introduced the No Sanctuary Cities Act, which would require state and local governments to cooperate with federal immigration authorities.

sources.title: Immigration : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter

Guns & Safety.stance: Carter continues to present himself as strongly pro-Second Amendment and aligned with gun-rights positions; no new official campaign policy language was found in the reviewed material.

sources.title: Buddy Carter For Senate
Georgia's Buddy Carter announces Senate candidacy against Jon Ossoff

Foreign Policy.stance: Took a hawkish position on Iran in March 2026; on March 5, 2026, he said he voted against H. Con. Res. 38 because Iran is a major terrorist threat, and he later praised Trump’s military action against Iran as addressing an imminent threat.

sources.title: National Defense : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter
Carter statement on vote in support of our troops against war powers resolution
ICYMI: Military action in Iran makes Americans safer - Buddy Carter

Social Justice.stance: Carter’s campaign messaging continues to emphasize Trump alignment, border security, and conservative culture-war themes rather than a distinct social-justice agenda.

sources.title: Buddy Carter For Senate
Georgia GOP U.S. Rep. Buddy Carter enters 2026 Senate race that shapes up as referendum on Trump policies

Education.stance: Carter continues to frame education around parental rights, school choice, and opposition to what he describes as government or union control of schools. In his education issue page, he says education decisions are best made by families, teachers, communities, and states. In a campaign email, he said parents’ rights in schools have been attacked, praised the House-passed Parents Bill of Rights, and said he supports school choice across public, charter, magnet, online, private, and home education options.

sources.title: Education : U.S. Representative Buddy Carter
School choice email from Representative Buddy Carter

Tech & AI.stance: Carter's most specific recent tech-related position is focused on child online safety rather than AI broadly. In December 2025, he promoted Sammy's Law, which would let parents use third-party safety software to monitor and protect children from dangerous interactions on social media.

sources.title: E&C holds hearing on Carter's bill protecting kids' online safety

Election Reform.stance: Carter is strongly supporting tighter election rules, including proof of citizenship and photo ID requirements for voting in federal elections; in February 2026 he publicly backed the SAVE America Act and argued it would strengthen election security and reduce fraud.

sources.title: WATCH: Carter defends election integrity, urges SAVE America Act passage on House floor
Carter lauds House passage of SAVE America Act
H.R.22 - SAVE Act

Local Issues.stance: Carter’s Georgia-facing messaging continues to focus on local cost-of-living and public-safety themes, with recent emphasis on immigration enforcement and community safety in coastal Georgia and Savannah-area concerns.

sources.title: Georgia lawmaker calls for ICE surge after Savannah death
As criticism of ICE mounts, Carter urges expansion to Georgia

candidates.name: Mike Collins
candidates.summary: Mike Collins is a Republican U.S. representative from Georgia’s 10th District and a candidate for the 2026 Senate race. He entered the contest in July 2025 and has since received outside support from conservative groups, including Club for Growth PAC, as he competes in the GOP primary.

Healthcare.stance: Recent official issue material on Collins' House website provides little detailed 2026 health-platform language, but his record in the 2026 Senate race includes opposition to renewing Affordable Care Act subsidies, with Republicans arguing the subsidies are too costly and support a broken system.

sources.title: Health | Representative Collins
Democrats criticize Georgia US Senate challengers for end of health insurance subsidies

Economy.stance: Collins continues to emphasize deregulation, lower costs for truckers and farmers, and opposition to federal rules he says burden businesses. In March 2026, his campaign and House materials highlighted his push to roll back EPA rules affecting diesel trucks and his broader message that federal regulation raises costs for working Georgians. His economic positioning also aligns with support for tariffs and other Trump-era trade policies.

sources.title: Press Releases | Representative Collins - Mike Collins - House.gov
Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!

Climate/Energy.stance: Continues to oppose federal emissions mandates and frames his energy position around reducing EPA regulation on trucks and farmers; in March 2026 he introduced the Diesel Truck Liberation Act to stop EPA emissions mandates.

sources.title: Press Releases | Representative Collins - Mike Collins - House.gov

Reproductive Rights.stance: Collins' record is anti-abortion based on his congressional voting history. Reproductive Freedom for All's scorecard lists him supporting multiple measures and amendments that it says restricted abortion access, including votes related to servicemembers' abortion access, mifepristone-related FDA actions, and the Born-Alive Abortion Survivors Protection Act.

sources.title: Mike Collins's Congressional Scorecard by Reproductive Freedom for All

Immigration.stance: Collins maintains a strong enforcement-first immigration position and has aligned himself with hard-line federal immigration enforcement themes in both his House and campaign messaging.

sources.title: Mike Collins - House.gov
Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!

Guns & Safety.stance: Collins remains publicly opposed to new gun restrictions and aligned with gun-rights positions; no newer campaign-specific policy language was found in the reviewed material.

sources.title: Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!
Georgia Republican Mike Collins seeks to challenge Democratic Sen. Jon Ossoff in 2026

Foreign Policy.stance: On March 4, 2026, Collins issued a statement on the Iran War Powers Resolution arguing that the Iranian regime's nuclear and missile programs and support for terrorist proxies pose serious threats, reflecting a hawkish posture on Iran.

sources.title: Senator Collins' Statement on Iran War Powers Resolution

Social Justice.stance: Collins has not articulated a clear social-justice platform in the reviewed campaign material; his messaging is centered on Trump, working-class themes, and conservative priorities.

sources.title: Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!
Mike Collins enters U.S. Senate race with pro-Trump agenda

Education.stance: Collins’ education position remains centered on parental rights and conservative oversight of schools. His House education page highlights support for the Parents Bill of Rights Act, and his campaign messaging continues to present him as backing parents’ control over children’s education rather than federal direction. No newer standalone education platform was identified in the latest materials reviewed.

sources.title: Education | Representative Collins
Mike Collins for Senate – Join the ride!

Tech & AI.stance: Available sourcing does not show a clearly defined AI-regulation platform from Collins. His record includes support for technology-related legislation such as the Deploying American Blockchains Act, while separate news coverage about his campaign's use of an AI-generated deepfake reflects campaign tactics rather than a stated policy position on AI governance.

sources.title: H.R. 6572: Deploying American Blockchains Act of 2023 - GovTrack.us
Georgia Rep. Mike Collins' campaign uses AI-generated deepfake of Senator Jon Ossoff in tight Senate showdown
AI deepfakes blur reality in 2026 US midterm campaigns

Election Reform.stance: Collins supports stricter election rules and voter-verification measures; in March 2026 he continued promoting voter ID and election-integrity messaging, including support for the SAVE Act and claims that tighter verification is needed to prevent noncitizen voting.

sources.title: Jon Ossoff is out there fighting for the votes of illegal immigrants...
Georgians overwhelmingly support voter ID...
H.R.22 - SAVE Act

Local Issues.stance: Collins’ latest Georgia-focused agenda centers on rural economic concerns, especially truckers and farmers, and on rolling back federal regulations he says raise costs for working Georgians; in March 2026 he introduced legislation targeting EPA diesel-emissions mandates.

sources.title: Representative Mike Collins Introduces the Diesel Truck Liberation Act
New bill aims to roll back diesel truck emissions mandates

candidates.name: Derek Dooley
candidates.summary: Derek Dooley is a Republican candidate for the 2026 U.S. Senate race in Georgia and a former University of Tennessee football coach. He launched his campaign in August 2025 and has recently rolled out a Georgia-focused policy pledge while seeking to build name recognition in the GOP field.

Derek Dooley - Volopedia

education.institution: University of Georgia School of Law

source.title: UGA School of Law Advocate 2010 Alumni

voting_record.bill_description: Dooley is a 2026 Senate candidate without prior congressional or state legislative service. AP reporting also noted that he had not voted for years before running.

source.title: Derek Dooley didn't vote for years. Now he wants to be a Georgia senator | AP News

Healthcare.stance: Reporting on the 2026 Senate race says Dooley opposed renewing Affordable Care Act subsidies, with Republicans arguing the subsidies are financially unsustainable and support a broken health care system. Reviewed campaign materials did not provide a more detailed health-policy platform.

sources.title: Democrats criticize Georgia US Senate challengers for end of health insurance subsidies
Derek Dooley announces 2026 Senate bid in Georgia

Economy.stance: Dooley’s economic message remains broad and Georgia-first rather than issue-specific. In March 2026 reporting on his Senate bid, he rolled out a campaign 'contract' and positioned himself as a reform-oriented outsider. Available coverage indicates he is emphasizing economic growth, taxes, and business-friendly reform, but not a detailed policy agenda.

sources.title: Daily Jolt: Derek Dooley rolls out 'contract' in U.S. Senate bid
Derek Dooley announces 2026 Senate bid in Georgia | AP News

Climate/Energy.stance: Dooley's campaign website and reviewed campaign materials do not provide a specific climate or energy policy platform. His current message is a broad pledge to work with President Trump and put Georgia first rather than a detailed energy agenda.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Daily Jolt: Derek Dooley rolls out 'contract' in U.S. Senate bid

Reproductive Rights.stance: Reviewed campaign materials do not present a detailed reproductive-rights platform from Dooley. Available public reporting reviewed for this profile focused more on his outsider message, ties to Trump, and accountability contract than on abortion or IVF policy specifics.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Derek Dooley didn’t vote for years. Now he wants to be a Georgia senator.

Immigration.stance: Dooley has said Georgia needs a secure border and that immigration laws should be enforced, but reviewed materials did not show a more detailed immigration platform.

sources.title: Derek Dooley didn't vote for years. Now he wants Georgia voters to send him to Washington.

Guns & Safety.stance: Dooley’s campaign site does not lay out a specific guns-and-safety policy in the reviewed material; his public messaging is broad and focused on Georgia-first leadership and support for Trump’s agenda.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Derek Dooley says beating Ossoff won't 'be easy' in senate race

Foreign Policy.stance: Dooley has signaled a hawkish posture toward Iran. In a 2026 campaign social-media post, he referenced watching the Iranian regime storm the U.S. embassy as a child and framed Iran as a longstanding threat.

sources.title: Derek Dooley X post on Iran

Social Justice.stance: Dooley’s Georgia First Contract uses broad language about respect and common sense, but the reviewed material does not show a detailed social-justice platform.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Daily Jolt: Derek Dooley rolls out 'contract' in U.S. Senate bid

Education.stance: Dooley still does not appear to have a detailed standalone education platform in the materials reviewed. Recent coverage and campaign materials reviewed for March 2026 did not identify specific education proposals beyond general Georgia-first and reform messaging.

sources.title: Derek Dooley speaks on 2026 campaign for Georgia State Senate
Derek Dooley announces 2026 Senate bid in Georgia | AP News

Tech & AI.stance: Dooley's campaign website and reviewed campaign materials do not provide a specific technology or AI policy platform.

sources.title: Derek Dooley for U.S. Senate
Daily Jolt: Derek Dooley rolls out 'contract' in U.S. Senate bid

Election Reform.stance: Dooley’s public profile remains relatively light on detailed election-reform proposals; recent coverage describes him as an outsider candidate focused more on accountability and broader campaign themes than on a specific voting-policy agenda.

sources.title: Derek Dooley didn't vote for years. Now he wants Georgia voters to send him to Washington.
Derek Dooley interview with The Georgia Vote

Local Issues.stance: Dooley is campaigning on Georgia-specific quality-of-life and community-improvement themes, saying he entered the race to improve people’s quality of life and uplift the communities he served; recent interviews suggest a focus on local concerns rather than a detailed policy platform.

sources.title: Derek Dooley speaks on 2026 campaign for Georgia State Senate
Derek Dooley talks about his run for Senate on The Means Report
Athens on the Ballot: Q&A with Derek Dooley, candidate for U.S. Senate

flags.concern: The top_donors data for Mike Collins (GA-10, elected 2022) is sourced from OpenSecrets CID N00035370, which belongs to Doug Collins (former GA-9 congressman, different person). This means all five donor entries likely reflect the wrong candidate's fundraising entirely.
flags.suggestion: Locate Mike Collins (GA-10)'s correct OpenSecrets CID by searching OpenSecrets.org directly or cross-referencing his bioguide ID (C001136). Re-pull all donor data using the correct CID and update the source URL and all five donor entries. This is a critical data-integrity error.

flags.concern: The FEC committee URL 'https://www.fec.gov/data/committee/C00544684/' is cited as Mike Collins' committee, but the CID should be verified as belonging to Collins (GA-10) and not confused with Doug Collins. The URL structure and CID should be independently confirmed.
flags.suggestion: Verify the committee ID C00544684 by searching FEC.gov for Mike Collins (GA-10) and confirming it matches his House campaign committee. If it does not, replace with the correct committee URL.

flags.concern: Jon Ossoff is recorded as voting 'no' on PN1522 (Jerome Powell Fed Chair nomination, May 12, 2022). Powell was confirmed 80–19 with overwhelming Democratic support. Ossoff voting no would be unusual and atypical for a Democrat on this nomination. This vote direction requires independent verification.
flags.suggestion: Check the official Senate roll-call for PN1522 on the Senate Clerk website or GovTrack. If Ossoff voted yes (as most Democrats did), correct the vote field. If he did vote no, add an explanatory note in the bill_description or update the source citation.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley's voting_record entry uses the date '2025-08-04' as a placeholder for a non-existent vote, with the note 'No legislative voting record identified.' Using an arbitrary date in a structured date field is misleading and violates data-integrity principles—it falsely implies a vote event occurred.
flags.suggestion: Remove or set the date field to null. The bill_name and bill_description clearly state no legislative record exists. Do not use placeholder dates for absent data.

flags.concern: Mike Collins' vote on H.R. 1917 (Great Lakes Mass Marking Program Act) is recorded with date '2025-07-22', which is a future date relative to the profile creation (March 29, 2026). While the 119th Congress may have been in session in July 2025, the bill number and title should be verified against GovTrack to ensure this is the correct vote record.
flags.suggestion: Cross-check the GovTrack page for Rep. Mike Collins (456895) to confirm H.R. 1917, its title, and the exact vote date. If the vote date and bill are correct, note should be added clarifying the legislative context.

flags.concern: The source URL 'https://www.ossoff.senate.gov/author/jm43521/' is an author archive page, not a specific press release on clean energy. This does not directly document Ossoff's climate or energy stance and is too vague to support even a 'medium' confidence claim.
flags.suggestion: Replace with a specific Ossoff Senate press release on clean energy, solar investment, or domestic manufacturing. If no such release can be located, lower confidence to 'low' and note that 2026 messaging on climate/energy is not prominently featured in reviewed materials.

flags.concern: The source for Ossoff's guns/safety stance points to his generic Five-Year Report rather than a specific statement on firearms legislation or gun safety. The stance description focuses on anti-trafficking work rather than directly addressing gun policy.
flags.suggestion: Locate a specific Ossoff Senate press release or voting record on gun legislation (e.g., background checks, safe storage, assault weapon restrictions). If none exists, lower confidence to 'low' and note that his 2026 messaging does not prominently feature direct gun-policy proposals.

flags.concern: The Capitol Beat source 'https://capitol-beat.org/2026/01/democrats-criticize-georgia-us-senate-challengers-for-end-of-health-insurance-subsidies/' is a news outlet reporting Democratic criticism of Republican positions rather than a direct statement from Carter himself. This is a secondary source summarizing opposition arguments and does not constitute primary documentation of Carter's own healthcare stance.
flags.suggestion: Replace with a direct source from Carter's campaign, House website, or official statement where he articulates his healthcare position (e.g., a press release on maternal health legislation or opposition to ACA subsidies). If only secondary reporting exists, lower confidence to 'medium' and note reliance on news coverage of his positions.

flags.concern: Buddy Carter's vote on H.R. 6572 (Deploying American Blockchains Act of 2023) is dated May 15, 2024. While the date appears plausible (this bill passed the House on that date), Carter's vote direction should be independently verified against the House Clerk roll-call record to confirm he voted yes.
flags.suggestion: Verify Carter's vote on H.R. 6572 (May 15, 2024) through the House Clerk's website or GovTrack. If the vote direction is incorrect, update accordingly.

flags.concern: The Tech & AI stance notes campaign use of an AI-generated deepfake (reported in CBS News and Reuters) and support for blockchain legislation, but explicitly states 'no clearly defined AI-regulation platform.' Confidence is marked 'low', which is appropriate, but the stance description leads with campaign tactics rather than policy absence.
flags.suggestion: Restructure the stance text to lead with 'Collins does not have a clearly articulated AI-regulation platform in reviewed materials.' Note the blockchain vote as the only relevant legislative action, and relegate the deepfake incident to a parenthetical about campaign tactics rather than policy. Maintain confidence at 'low'.

flags.concern: The source URL 'http://collins.house.gov/media/press-releases' is a generic landing page listing all press releases, not a specific document. This does not meet the sourcing standard applied elsewhere in the profile where specific press releases are linked.
flags.suggestion: Locate the specific Collins press release or House page on the Diesel Truck Liberation Act or EPA deregulation and link directly to that document. If the specific URL cannot be found, use the next-best available specific source (e.g., a news article on the legislation).

flags.concern: The source URL 'http://collins.house.gov/media/press-releases' is again a generic landing page rather than specific documentation of Collins' local-issues stance. This fails to support the claim with direct sourcing.
flags.suggestion: Replace with specific press releases on the Diesel Truck Liberation Act, or other targeted Georgia-specific economic or regulatory initiatives. If Collins has no detailed local-issues press releases, lower confidence and note the lack of specific campaign material on local concerns.

flags.concern: The source URL for Mike Collins' Iran statement is listed as 'https://www.collins.senate.gov/newsroom/03/04/2026/senator-collins-statement-on-iran-war-powers-resolution', but Mike Collins is a House member, not a senator. This URL domain (.senate.gov) is incorrect and suggests a data error or fabrication.
flags.suggestion: Locate the correct press release on Collins' House website (collins.house.gov) or confirm the date and content of his Iran statement. If the statement does not exist, remove this source and lower confidence accordingly, or replace with an alternative verified source.

flags.concern: Derek Dooley's top_donors array is empty despite the summary noting he raised $3 million (July–December 2025) and had $2.14 million cash on hand. FEC itemized receipts for this period should identify top donors above $200, creating an information gap compared to other candidates.
flags.suggestion: Query FEC.gov using Dooley's candidate ID (S6GA00408) to retrieve his campaign committee's itemized receipts for July–December 2025. Populate top_donors with the five largest itemized contributors and cite the specific FEC filing (e.g., 2025 Year-End Report).

flags.concern: Dooley's healthcare stance is sourced to news reporting of Democratic criticism rather than a direct Dooley statement or campaign document. The confidence is 'medium', but the source does not directly document his position.
flags.suggestion: Search Dooley's campaign website (dooleyforgeorgia.com) and social media for direct statements on healthcare, ACA subsidies, or prescription drug prices. If no direct sources exist, lower confidence to 'low' and note that his healthcare platform has not been publicly detailed.

flags.concern: Dooley's reproductive-rights stance is marked as 'low' confidence with a note that 'campaign materials do not present a detailed reproductive-rights platform.' This is honest, but it highlights a significant completeness gap for a major campaign issue.
flags.suggestion: Conduct targeted searches of Dooley's campaign website, recent social media posts, and local Georgia news interviews for any statement on abortion, IVF, or contraception access. If truly no position has been articulated, maintain 'low' confidence and explicitly note in the stance that Dooley has not detailed a reproductive-rights platform to voters.

flags.concern: Dooley's education stance is marked 'low' confidence with the note that 'recent coverage and campaign materials reviewed for March 2026 did not identify specific education proposals.' This is honest but represents a significant gap for a major issue area.
flags.suggestion: Search Dooley's campaign website and local Georgia news outlets (Atlanta Journal-Constitution, WABE, local TV stations) for any education-related statements. If none exist, maintain 'low' confidence and add a note that his education platform remains undeveloped or not yet publicly detailed.

flags.concern: Dooley's Tech & AI stance note that 'campaign website and reviewed campaign materials do not provide a specific technology or AI policy platform.' With 'medium' confidence, this gap is acknowledged but the candidate has no articulated position.
flags.suggestion: Search Dooley's campaign site and social media one more time for any tech or AI comments. If none exist, lower confidence to 'low' and note explicitly that a technology policy platform has not been publicly articulated.

flags.concern: Dooley's election-reform stance is marked 'low' confidence with a note that his 'public profile remains relatively light on detailed election-reform proposals.' This is honest but represents a gap in a significant policy area.
flags.suggestion: Search Dooley's campaign site, social media, and recent news interviews for any position on voter ID, election security, or voting access. If none found, maintain 'low' confidence and note that his election-reform platform has not been publicly detailed.

flags.concern: The race description notes the Republican field 'could still change' but does not mention Georgia's May 2026 primary date or the state's runoff rules, which are material to understanding how the race will unfold (if no candidate clears 50% in the primary, a runoff occurs).
flags.suggestion: Add a sentence: 'Georgia holds its primary election in May 2026; if no Republican candidate wins 50% of the primary vote, the top two finishers will compete in a July runoff.' This provides context on the race structure.

flags.concern: Only one poll (Emerson College, March 2–5, 2026) is included for a major battleground Senate race. The iteration notes acknowledge sparse polling availability, but this remains an unusual situation for a profile updated in late March 2026.
flags.suggestion: Conduct a final search for any other publicly released Georgia Senate polls from early 2026 (Quinnipiac, Fox News, local outlets). If truly only Emerson's poll is available, add an explicit note to the polling section: 'As of late March 2026, public polling for this race is sparse. The Emerson poll is the only publicly released survey located; additional polls may emerge as the election approaches.'

flags.concern: The FEC committee URL for Buddy Carter (C00543967) should be independently verified to ensure it is the correct committee ID for his House campaign and not mismatched to a different candidate.
flags.suggestion: Verify C00543967 on FEC.gov by searching for Buddy Carter (candidate ID N00035346). If C00543967 is his correct committee, the source is fine; if not, replace with the correct committee URL.

flags.concern: Mike Collins' education lists a B.A. in Business from the University of Georgia (1990), but some biographical sources may indicate uncertainty about the degree field or completion status. This should be verified.
flags.suggestion: Cross-check Collins' education against the Congressional Biographical Directory (bioguide C001136) and any official campaign biography. If uncertainty exists, either confirm or add a note on the sourcing limitation.

flags.concern: Ossoff's vote on H.J.Res. 20 (Congressional Review Act disapproval) dated April 10, 2025, lacks specific identification of which federal rule is being disapproved. The bill_description says 'a federal rule' but does not name it.
flags.suggestion: Identify the specific rule H.J.Res. 20 disapproves and update bill_description (e.g., 'H.J.Res. 20: Congressional Review Act disapproval of [rule name]'). Verify the vote date and direction against Senate roll-call records.

reviews.summary: This is a thoughtful and well-intentioned profile that demonstrates genuine editorial care: sourcing consistently relies on official government records (bioguide, FEC, Congress.gov, GovTrack) and primary materials (Senate/House press releases); issue stances are properly attributed and confidence levels are honestly calibrated; gaps are disclosed rather than obscured. The iteration notes show systematic effort to verify and correct the record. However, there are several substantive factual and data-integrity issues that require correction before approval: FEC committee IDs appear mismatched to candidates (Mike Collins linked to Doug Collins' data); a critical voting-record entry uses an implausible future date and placeholder; multiple source URLs point to generic landing pages rather than specific documents; and completeness gaps for Derek Dooley remain significant. These are correctable problems, but they affect reliability on key facts.

flags.concern: The OpenSecrets URL uses CID N00035370, which corresponds to Doug Collins, not Mike Collins (GA-10), leading to potentially inaccurate donor data.
flags.suggestion: Verify and update with the correct CID for Mike Collins on OpenSecrets.org and FEC.gov, then repopulate the top_donors array with accurate 2023-2026 cycle data.

flags.concern: The array is empty despite evidence of significant fundraising (e.g., $3 million in 2025), indicating a completeness gap in donor information.
flags.suggestion: Query FEC.gov for Derek Dooley's filings (candidate ID S6GA00408) and add top donors from itemized receipts, citing specific sources.

flags.concern: Jon Ossoff is recorded as voting 'no' on PN1522 (Jerome Powell's nomination), which is atypical for a Democrat and inconsistent with the 80-19 confirmation vote.
flags.suggestion: Cross-check Senate roll-call records on congress.gov to confirm Ossoff's vote and correct if necessary (e.g., to 'yes').

flags.concern: The stance for Derek Dooley is vague and lacks specific policy details, relying on broad campaign themes rather than sourced positions.
flags.suggestion: Expand with any available campaign statements or news sources; if none exist, note the limitation explicitly to maintain completeness.

flags.concern: Only one poll is included, which limits completeness for a competitive race; this may not reflect the full landscape as of late March 2026.
flags.suggestion: Search for additional polls from credible sources like Quinnipiac or Fox News and add them if available, or note in the description that polling is sparse.

flags.concern: The stance conflates campaign behavior (e.g., AI deepfake use) with legislative activity, potentially misrepresenting Mike Collins' policy position.
flags.suggestion: Reframe to clearly separate campaign tactics from policy, emphasizing the absence of a defined AI-regulation platform and retaining 'low' confidence.

reviews.summary: This profile demonstrates thorough research and neutral language across most candidate sections, with strong sourcing for Jon Ossoff and Buddy Carter, but it still requires revisions for factual accuracy in voting records (e.g., potential date errors and vote verifications), completeness in Derek Dooley's issue stances and donor data, and source specificity to address generic URLs and gaps in polling.
